macro_segments.title: Introduction and Review of Arayos for Bnei Noach: The Dispute between Rabbi Akiva and Rabbi Eliezer

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and context: yesterday's discussion on retzicha, fetuses, and Bnei Noach
Women's status in Sheva Mitzvos Bnei Noach and the role of single witness without hasra'ah
Review of marriage and arayos: beulat ba'al vs. erusin for Bnei Noach
Rabbi Akiva vs. Rabbi Eliezer on the scope of arayos: death penalty vs. sisterhood principle

macro_segments.title: The Case of Adam and Kayin: Did Adam Marry His Daughter and What Does It Tell Us About Sister Relationships

micro_segments.title: Tashma: Why Adam did not marry his daughter—set aside for Kayin to marry his sister
The hermeneutic tension: Did the allowance for Kayin indicate a general rule or special exception?
Rashi on the word 'chesed' in the parsha of arayos and its connection to Kayin
Rav Huna's position: Kuti mutar bevito (daughter permitted) based on narrow reading of pasuk
Objection and reconciliation: How Rav Huna's reading accommodates the story of Adam
Alternative opinions and summary of three positions on daughter prohibition

macro_segments.title: The Special Case of the Converting Slave: Neither Bnei Noach nor Yisrael

micro_segments.title: Rav Chisda on mutar be'imo and bevito (mother and daughter) for a slave
The principle of k'katan shenolad dami: the slave as quasi-newborn Jew
The paradox: Jewish law applies yet relations are not recognized; future children would be prohibited
Discussion of the ambiguous status of a slave and concerns about leniency perceptions

macro_segments.title: Marriage and Divorce for Bnei Noach: The Concept of Yichud and De Facto Marriage

micro_segments.title: Ben Noach designates maidservant for his slave; intercourse with her results in execution
Yichud (designation) as the defining moment of marriage for enslaved individuals
Rav Nachman on when yichud begins: societal recognition through naming conventions
When does marriage end for slaves: uncovering hair in public as sign of dissolution
Rambam on divorce for Bnei Noach: either party can initiate separation; no get required
Contrast with Jewish marriage: beulat ba'al as de facto cohabitation vs. formal kiddushin
Modern discussion: Some advocate for Jewish marriage without kiddushin to address aguna problem

macro_segments.title: Intercourse with Another Man's Wife: The Restriction on 'Not According to the Way'

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Chanina: Bnei Noach prohibited from unnatural intercourse with their own wives
The pasuk on ve'davak (cleaving) and Rashi's explanation: emotional adhesion requires pleasure
Rava's distinction: The issue is not intercourse with one's own wife, but with another man's wife
The narrow definition: unnatural intercourse with another man's wife is not considered transgression
Implications for shiurim doctrine: Bnei Noach have formal limits on what constitutes prohibited acts

macro_segments.title: Violence and Transgression: Hitting, Slapping, and the Status of the Wicked

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Chanina: A non-Jew who hits a Jew is chayav mita; support from Moshe and Egyptian
Slapping the cheek as slapping the Divine Presence; derasha from mokash adam
Reish Lakish on lifting hand against another: rasha status even without actual striking
Zeeira's alternative: chotei (sinner) rather than rasha; varying degrees of culpability
Rav Huna on cutting off the hand: extrajudicial punishment and the principle shelo min hadin
Tosafot vs. Rashi: Is this true din or extraordinary punishment?

macro_segments.title: Labor and Rest: Non-Jews Prohibited from Shabbat Observance

micro_segments.title: Reish Lakish: Non-Jew who observes Shabbat is chayav mita
The pasuk yom v'laila lo yishbotu: day and night cannot cease; non-Jews must always work
Ravina: Even on a weekday (Monday, not Shabbat), observing rest is problematic
Rashi's reading: Both religious and secular rest are prohibited; no day off permitted
Why not count Shabbat among Sheva Mitzvos Bnei Noach? Violation is passive, not active
Question on Jewish slaves: Do they get Shabbat rest despite Bnei Noach prohibition?
Dinim as exception: has both active (corrupt judgment) and passive (failure) components
Rambam's interpretation: Problem is religious day-keeping, not secular rest; connects to next topic

macro_segments.title: Learning Torah and Creating New Religions: Restrictions on Non-Jewish Religious Practice

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan: Non-Jew who engages in Torah is chayav mita; Torah is inheritance to us only
Drashot on morasha: Reading as stealing (gezel) vs. reading as betrothal/marriage to Torah
Rabbi Meir: Non-Jew engaged in Torah is like Kohen Gadol; pasuk 'adam asher ya'aseh otam v'chai bahem'
Gemara reconciliation: Applies only when learning within context of Sheva Mitzvos Bnei Noach
Broader context: Collection of universalist statements (goy tzaddik, ויבא גוי צדיק)
The significance of Sheva Mitzvos Bnei Noach as unique religious framework allowing non-Jews righteous path

macro_segments.title: Ever Min Hachai and Dam Min Hachai: Which Prohibitions Apply to Whom?

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Chanina ben Gamliel: Dam min hachai (blood from living animal) also prohibited for Bnei Noach
Derasha from 'ach basar benafsho damo lo tocheilu': Both flesh and blood while alive
Tanu Rabbanan: Dam min hachai is distinct; blood considered separate from flesh only in certain animals
Why dam min hachai is not separate issue: 'dam hu hanefesh' (blood is the soul) applies to lifeblood only

macro_segments.title: The Principle of Repetition: What Was Said to Bnei Noach and Repeated at Sinai

micro_segments.title: Lemah li lemichtav le'Bnei Noach—Why repeat at Sinai if already given to Bnei Noach?
Application to Ever Min Hachai: Must be repeated to confirm it applies to both Bnei Noach and Yisrael
Rabbi Yosi be'Rabbi Chanina's principle: Said to Bnei Noach and repeated at Sinai applies to both
The counterintuitive rule: Said to Bnei Noach but NOT repeated applies only to Yisrael
The one exception: Gid hanasheh according to Rabbi Yehuda—said at time of Yaakov, not repeated
Objection: Wouldn't logic suggest unrepeated laws apply only to Bnei Noach?
Proof from avodah zara: Repeated at Sinai and both Bnei Noach and Yisrael are punished for it
Principle of non-contradiction: Cannot be something permitted to Yisrael but forbidden to Bnei Noach

topical_tags.term: Sheva Mitzvos Bnei Noach (seven laws for non-Jews)
Rabbi Akiva vs. Rabbi Eliezer dispute
Beulat ba'al (de facto marriage through intercourse)
Yichud (designation of woman as wife for slaves)
K'katan shenolad dami (quasi-newborn status upon conversion)
Lifnei iver (not causing a blind person to stumble; not assisting in transgression)
Ever min hachai (limb from living animal)
Dam min hachai (blood from living animal)
Repetition principle (said to Bnei Noach and repeated at Sinai)
Rambam on non-Jewish marriage and divorce
Rav Huna on law and punishment
